title: Content Addressing & User Consent
description: The R3 document's display section drives informed user consent — the resource describes what IT does, including irreversible actions. The agent's Person Server (PS) fetches the R3 document and presents it to the user before approving. The r3_s256 hash baked into the final auth token creates a permanent, content-addressed audit record: even if the resource later updates the document at the same URI, the approved semantics are pinned forever.
spec_section: R3 §5 — R3 Document, §6 — Auth Token Extensions

steps.label: POST /authorize + r3_operations → resource token (r3_uri + r3_s256)
annotations: Agent requests two MCP tools: create_calendar_event and send_calendar_invite.
Resource maps these to an R3 document. This R3 document has a display.irreversible field — sent invitations cannot be recalled.
The resource token carries r3_uri (where the doc lives) and r3_s256 (SHA-256 of its content).
aud=PS URL in the resource token — this is a 3-party flow. The PS is the token authority and will handle consent.
The agent cannot read the R3 document. It only carries the hash — 'agent opacity'.

steps.label: POST /token + resource token → 202 deferred (user consent required)

response_body.detail: User consent required — R3 document contains irreversible actions

annotations: Agent sends the resource token to its PS. PS sees aud=its own URL — it is the sole token authority (3-party).
PS sees r3_uri and r3_s256 in the resource token. It needs to fetch the R3 document to evaluate policy and show consent.
PS returns 202 Accepted with a pending URL (for polling) and an interaction URL (for the user to visit and consent).
PS fetches the R3 doc in the background (step 3) to populate the consent UI before the user arrives.

steps.label: GET /r3/a1b2c3d4 → R3 document with display fields for consent

display.summary: Create events and send meeting invitations on your work calendar
display.implications: New calendar events will be created. Meeting invitations will be sent to specified attendees from your calendar identity.
display.data_accessed: Event titles, times, attendee email addresses, and calendar identity (display name and email)
display.irreversible: Sent meeting invitations cannot be unsent. Recipients will receive email notifications immediately upon event creation.

display.summary: Create events and send meeting invitations on your work calendar
display.implications: New calendar events will be created. Meeting invitations will be sent to specified attendees from your calendar identity.
display.data_accessed: Event titles, times, attendee email addresses, and calendar identity (display name and email)
display.irreversible: Sent meeting invitations cannot be unsent. Recipients will receive email notifications immediately upon event creation.

annotations: PS fetches the R3 document to populate the consent UI. Agents cannot fetch this endpoint — only the PS (or AS in 4-party).
PS verifies SHA-256(canonical JSON) == r3_s256 from the resource token. Mismatch → reject.
The display section is written by the resource (what IT does), not the agent (what it wants).
display.irreversible is the key field: PS uses this to require explicit user approval before proceeding.
Cache-Control: immutable — this URI is content-addressed. If the resource ever changes this document, it will be at a new URI with a new hash.

steps.label: User reviews consent (irreversible warning shown) → approves

response_body.r3_summary: Create events and send meeting invitations on your work calendar
response_body.r3_implications: Meeting invitations will be sent to specified attendees from your calendar identity.
response_body.r3_data_accessed: Event titles, times, attendee email addresses, and calendar identity
response_body.r3_irreversible: ⚠️ Sent meeting invitations cannot be unsent

annotations: PS renders the consent page using the display fields from the R3 document.
The irreversible warning is prominently shown: 'Sent meeting invitations cannot be unsent'.
User sees exactly what the resource itself declared — not what the agent claims it will do.
User approves. The PS can now issue the auth token.

steps.label: Poll /pending/... → 200 auth token (r3_s256 = immutable audit record)
annotations: After user approval, the next poll returns 200 with the PS-issued auth token (iss=PS in 3-party).
AUDIT: r3_uri + r3_s256 are baked into the auth token. This is a permanent, verifiable record of which R3 document was in effect at approval time.
If the resource later updates the document at the same URI, existing tokens still reference the previous version's hash.
Anyone with this token can verify the exact authorization semantics by fetching r3_uri and checking SHA-256 == r3_s256.
The PS audit log records r3_uri + r3_s256 alongside the agent identifier, user identity, and timestamp — a complete, unforgeable record.

events.label: Issued by PS after user consent
Deferred — consent required
events.detail: PS needs user approval. R3 doc has irreversible action: sent invitations cannot be recalled.

events.label: Approved — auth token issued
events.detail: User reviewed the R3 display fields and approved. Auth token includes r3_s256 as immutable audit record.
